pattern: Sort a linked list in n-log-n time by recursively splitting it at the midpoint and merging the two sorted halves back together.
hints: Comparison sorting in n log n on a list points at merge sort, which only needs sequential access — exactly what a singly linked list offers.
Split the list into two halves using slow/fast pointers (cutting the link before the slow pointer's stop), recursively sort each half, then merge the two sorted halves.
Merge with a dummy head and a tail pointer, attaching the smaller current node each step; the base case is an empty or single-node list, which is already sorted.

stress.description: long random list across the full value range

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def sortList(self, head):
        if not head or not head.next:
            return head
        slow, fast = head, head.next
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        mid = slow.next
        slow.next = None
        left = self.sortList(head)
        right = self.sortList(mid)
        dummy = ListNode(0)
        tail = dummy
        while left and right:
            if left.val <= right.val:
                tail.next = left
                left = left.next
            else:
                tail.next = right
                right = right.next
            tail = tail.next
        tail.next = left if left else right
        return dummy.next
